turns.content: quick question about Excel. I've got a VLOOKUP situation that's driving me insane
Hit me with it — what's the setup and what's going wrong?

okay so I've got two sheets. Sheet 1 has a list of entity codes and their corresponding revenue figures. Sheet 2 has the same entity codes but I need to pull in the revenue AND a status column from Sheet 1. the basic VLOOKUP works fine for the revenue column but I need to do a second VLOOKUP for the status and my manager said I should nest them instead of having two separate columns with two separate VLOOKUPs? I don't even know what that means tbh

Okay so your manager might be thinking of one of two things, and honestly the simpler interpretation is probably what they mean:

**Option 1: Two separate VLOOKUPs (totally fine)**
One column pulls revenue, another pulls status. Each has its own VLOOKUP formula pointing to Sheet 1. This is clean, readable, and there's nothing wrong with it:

```
=VLOOKUP(A2, Sheet1!$A:$D, 2, FALSE)   ← for revenue (column 2)
=VLOOKUP(A2, Sheet1!$A:$D, 3, FALSE)   ← for status (column 3)
```

**Option 2: Nested VLOOKUP (using one inside another)**
This is when the column index number itself comes from a lookup — it's more advanced and honestly rarely needed for what you're describing.

What column numbers are revenue and status in Sheet 1? And how is the data laid out — is the entity code in column A?

yeah entity code is column A on both sheets. revenue is column C on Sheet 1 and status is column E. I've already got the revenue VLOOKUP working in a separate column on Sheet 2 so that's fine. but he specifically said "nest them" and I don't want to go back and ask what he meant loool

Ha, totally get that. Okay so he might mean one of these things:

**1. He wants you to use INDEX-MATCH instead of VLOOKUP** — some people call this "nesting" because you're nesting MATCH inside INDEX. This is actually better practice than VLOOKUP because it doesn't care about column order:

```
=INDEX(Sheet1!$C:$C, MATCH(A2, Sheet1!$A:$A, 0))   ← revenue
=INDEX(Sheet1!$E:$E, MATCH(A2, Sheet1!$A:$A, 0))   ← status
```

**2. He wants you to combine both results into one cell** using something like:

```
=VLOOKUP(A2, Sheet1!$A:$E, 3, FALSE) & " — " & VLOOKUP(A2, Sheet1!$A:$E, 5, FALSE)
```

That would give you something like "£50,000 — Active" in a single cell.

My guess? He probably means option 1 — INDEX-MATCH. It's what anyone who's been in finance or audit for a while would consider the proper way to do lookups.
wallah I think you're right it's the INDEX MATCH thing. he's always going on about how VLOOKUP is outdated. let me try that. so the MATCH part finds the row and INDEX pulls the value from that row?

Exactly right. Think of it like this:

- **MATCH(A2, Sheet1!$A:$A, 0)** says: "Find my entity code in Sheet 1's column A and tell me what row number it's in." The 0 means exact match.
- **INDEX(Sheet1!$C:$C, [row number])** says: "Go to column C of Sheet 1 and give me the value at that row number."

So INDEX-MATCH is basically: "Find where this entity code lives, then go grab the value I need from that row." You swap out the column reference (C for revenue, E for status) depending on what you want to pull.

The big advantage over VLOOKUP: your lookup column doesn't have to be the leftmost column, and you can insert/delete columns without breaking everything.

okay that actually makes so much sense when you explain it like that. I've been avoiding INDEX MATCH for like a year because it looked complicated but it's literally just two steps. let me try it hold on
Take your time — once it clicks, you'll never go back to VLOOKUP. Just make sure you lock the ranges with dollar signs ($) so it doesn't shift when you drag the formula down.
RIGHT okay it's working. I did =INDEX(Sheet1!$E:$E, MATCH(A2, Sheet1!$A:$A, 0)) for the status column and it's pulling the right values. dragged it down and everything looks correct. I feel powerful

Love that energy. You've just levelled up your spreadsheet game permanently. Quick tip: if you ever get a #N/A error on some rows, it means MATCH couldn't find that entity code in Sheet 1 — usually a trailing space or formatting difference. You can wrap it in IFERROR to handle those gracefully:

```
=IFERROR(INDEX(Sheet1!$E:$E, MATCH(A2, Sheet1!$A:$A, 0)), "Not Found")
```

That way instead of ugly #N/A errors you get a clean "Not Found" message.

oh that's smart yeah there's actually 2 rows showing #N/A. probably entities that got removed or something. I'll add the IFERROR wrapper. thanks so much this was bare useful, my manager's gonna think I've been doing INDEX MATCH for years loool
That's the goal — look like you've known it forever. And honestly after doing it once it becomes second nature. Good luck with the rest of the workbook!

actually wait sorry one more thing. is there a way to do INDEX MATCH but match on TWO columns? like if I needed to match on entity code AND a date? because some entities appear multiple times with different months

Great question — yes! That's a common situation with financial data. You've got two main options:

**1. CTRL+SHIFT+ENTER array formula (older Excel):**
```
=INDEX(Sheet1!$C:$C, MATCH(1, (Sheet1!$A:$A=A2)*(Sheet1!$B:$B=B2), 0))
```
You have to press Ctrl+Shift+Enter instead of just Enter — it wraps the formula in curly braces.

**2. If you have Microsoft 365 or Excel 2021+, use XLOOKUP with a concatenation trick:**
```
=XLOOKUP(A2&B2, Sheet1!$A:$A&Sheet1!$B:$B, Sheet1!$C:$C)
```

The array formula version works everywhere but can be slow on huge datasets. If you're on 365 then XLOOKUP is cleaner. Which version of Excel are you on?
I think we've got 365 at work because we have the cloud stuff. I'll try the XLOOKUP one, that looks way cleaner. honestly this is dead helpful I'm saving this entire chat for future reference loool

Haha do it — future you will thank you. With 365 you've got all the modern functions so honestly XLOOKUP should be your go-to from now on, even over INDEX-MATCH. It's basically Microsoft admitting VLOOKUP was flawed and building the replacement. Right, go smash that workbook — you've levelled up twice in one conversation!
